---
title: "Introduction"
Author: "Mikkel E. Steentoft"
format: html
editor: visual
bibliography: references.bib
csl: acta-diabetologica.csl
---

The global prevalence of type 2 diabetes (T2D), prediabetes and associated complications continues to increase, indicating that existing prevention and treatment strategies are insufficient in stopping the progression of the disease (1, 2), and this may reflect that T2D is a more heterogeneous disease than previously assumed. Consequently, data-driven clustering approaches have recently identified distinct subgroups with different pathological characteristics and risk-trajectories for diabetes-related complications (2-4). Emerging evidence suggests that this heterogeneity is already present among individuals with prediabetes (5-7), traditionally defined by impaired fasting glucose and impaired glucose tolerance, which arise from a combination of declining beta-cell function and insulin resistance (8)

Studies have identified subgroups among individuals at high risk of T2D based on distinct glucose response patterns during a 3-point oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT), suggesting that dynamic glucose measures can further refine risk stratification. Beyond glucose responses, recent evidence indicates that dynamic hormonal responses to the OGTT, specifically those related to glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1), gastric inhibitory polypeptide (GIP) and glucagon may play an important role in progression to T2D. Moreover, it has recently been shown that body fat distribution varies across subtypes of individuals with prediabetes, with distinct patterns of visceral and hepatic fat influencing risk trajectories. However, the dynamic responses of glucose, insulin, incretins and glucagon during the OGTT, and how different patterns of fat distribution may be associated with these responses, remain unclear. Understanding these associations could improve identification of high-risk populations and facilitate targeted prevention strategies.

1.            Saeedi P, Petersohn I, Salpea P, Malanda B, Karuranga S, Unwin N, et al. Global and regional diabetes prevalence estimates for 2019 and projections for 2030 and 2045: Results from the International Diabetes Federation Diabetes Atlas, 9th edition. Diabetes Research and Clinical Practice. 2019;157:107843.

2.            Ahlqvist E, Storm P, Käräjämäki A, Martinell M, Dorkhan M, Carlsson A, et al. Novel subgroups of adult-onset diabetes and their association with outcomes: a data-driven cluster analysis of six variables. Lancet Diabetes Endocrinol. 2018;6(5):361-9.

3.            Misra S, Wagner R, Ozkan B, Schön M, Sevilla-Gonzalez M, Prystupa K, et al. Precision subclassification of type 2 diabetes: a systematic review. Commun Med (Lond). 2023;3(1):138.

4.            Li B, Yang Z, Liu Y, Zhou X, Wang W, Gao Z, et al. Clinical characteristics and complication risks in data-driven clusters among Chinese community diabetes populations. J Diabetes. 2024;16(8):e13596.

5.            Wagner R, Selvin E, Sehgal R, Prystupa K, Misra S, Fritsche A, et al. Beyond Glucose—Rethinking Prediabetes for Precision Prevention. Diabetes Care. 2025.

6.            Onthoni DD, Chen YE, Lai YH, Li GH, Zhuang YS, Lin HM, et al. Clustering-based risk stratification of prediabetes populations: Insights from the Taiwan and UK Biobanks. J Diabetes Investig. 2025;16(1):25-35.

7.            Wagner R, Heni M, Tabák AG, Machann J, Schick F, Randrianarisoa E, et al. Pathophysiology-based subphenotyping of individuals at elevated risk for type 2 diabetes. Nature Medicine. 2021;27(1):49-57.

8.            Echouffo-Tcheugui JB, Selvin E, Fielding JE. Prediabetes and What It Means: The Epidemiological Evidence. Annual review of public health. 2021;42(1):59-77.
